ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Korean boneless pa dak; closest match is the generic fried chicken food product class. The spring onion topping and Korean-style double-fry preparation are not captured by this term.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00GJ covers fried chicken meat generically. Korean-style preparation with spring onion topping and soy-garlic dipping sauce is not distinguished at this level of the FoodEx2 hierarchy.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for Korean fried chicken dish; SNOMED CT does not typically encode prepared ethnic dish variants at this specificity.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of Boneless Pa Dak is a large shared plate of approximately 500 g, intended for 2–3 people, yielding roughly 165–250 g per person depending on accompaniments.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Chicken, broilers or fryers, meat only, fried (SR Legacy ID 05062); supplemented with HPB Singapore Nutrient Composition of Food for Korean fried chicken analogues
nutrient_source.organization: U.S. Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service; Health Promotion Board, Singapore
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact USDA or HPB entry exists for Korean-style boneless pa dak. Values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy fried chicken (meat only, fried, SR ID 05062) and cross-referenced with HPB Singapore data for Korean fried chicken analogues. The wheat-flour/cornstarch batter increases carbohydrate content relative to plain fried chicken; sodium is elevated by soy-based marinade and dipping sauce. Input record values (energy 310 kcal, protein 21 g, fat 20 g, carbohydrate 11 g, sodium 560 mg) are consistent with this adapted estimate and are retained with medium confidence.

gi_source.title: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283. — Analogous estimate based on battered/breaded fried chicken (GI ~65, medium category)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Korean boneless pa dak. GI of 65 is estimated from the closest analogous food: battered/breaded fried chicken, which appears in the University of Sydney GI Database and the 2008 International Tables (Atkinson et al., Diabetes Care 2008). The wheat-flour and cornstarch batter is the primary carbohydrate source. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (65) × carbohydrate per serving (g) / 100. For a conservative 125 g individual portion: carbohydrate = 11 g/100 g × 125 g = 13.75 g; GL = 65 × 13.75 / 100 ≈ 8.9, reported as 8.0. Medium GI category.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 560 mg/100 g from soy-based marinade and dipping sauce, with insufficient potassium offset
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid Boneless Pa Dak due to its high sodium content of 560 mg/100 g; request reduced or low-sodium soy sauce in both the marinade and dipping sauce, and consume only a small portion infrequently.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Refined carbohydrate batter (11 g/100 g) with low dietary fibre (0.6 g/100 g) and medium GI (~65), limiting glucose moderation
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should consume Boneless Pa Dak in small portions only, as the refined wheat-flour and cornstarch batter provides 11 g carbohydrate/100 g with minimal fibre (0.6 g/100 g) and a medium GI of approximately 65; pairing with non-starchy vegetables and avoiding the sugar-containing dipping sauce will help moderate postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Deep-frying contributes 20 g total fat/100 g including 5 g saturated fat and 0.1 g trans fat, alongside high sodium (560 mg/100 g) and cholesterol (90 mg/100 g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should avoid or strictly limit Boneless Pa Dak due to its combination of high total fat (20 g/100 g), saturated fat at the 5 g/100 g threshold, trans fat (0.1 g/100 g), cholesterol (90 mg/100 g), and high sodium (560 mg/100 g), all of which adversely affect lipid profiles and blood pressure; grilled or baked preparation with reduced soy sauce is a preferable alternative.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-frying generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) that directly promote joint inflammation; saturated fat at 5 g/100 g threshold
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid Boneless Pa Dak as deep-frying generates AGEs that worsen joint inflammation; request grilled or baked preparation and reduce soy sauce to lower sodium-driven tissue swelling.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Boneless Pa Dak (뼈없는 파닭) is a Korean-style crispy boneless fried chicken dish distinguished by a generous topping of fresh spring onions (pa, 파), which give the dish its name. The chicken pieces are marinated in a soy-garlic base, coated in a light wheat-flour and cornstarch batter, and deep-fried until golden and crispy. The dish is typically served with a soy-garlic or mustard-based dipping sauce. In Singapore, it is commonly sold as a large shared plate (approximately 500 g) suitable for 2–3 people, often as part of a Korean fried chicken (KFC) restaurant menu. Nutritionally, per 100 g it provides approximately 310 kcal, 21 g protein, 20 g fat (5 g saturated, 0.1 g trans), 11 g carbohydrate, 0.6 g dietary fibre, and 560 mg sodium. The high sodium content (driven by soy sauce in both marinade and dipping sauce) makes it unsuitable for individuals with hypertension. The refined carbohydrate batter and medium glycemic index (~65) require portion control for people with Type 2 Diabetes. The combination of deep-frying, saturated fat, trans fat, and high sodium presents a high cardiovascular disease concern. The deep-frying process also generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs), making it unsuitable for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis. The spring onion topping provides minor antioxidant benefit but does not meaningfully offset the clinical concerns of the preparation method.
common_user_queries: Is pa dak suitable for diabetics?

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Korean boneless pa dak exists in USDA FoodData Central or the HPB Singapore database. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy fried chicken (meat only, fried) with adjustments for the wheat-flour/cornstarch batter and soy-based marinade. Input record values are consistent with this adapted estimate. GI is an analogous estimate from battered fried chicken in the University of Sydney GI Database. Medium confidence reflects the absence of a direct approved-source match.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, B-vitamin profile, fat-soluble vitamins, and moisture data for Korean-style boneless pa dak are not available in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (ID 05062) or the HPB Singapore Nutrient Composition of Food database at the level of specificity required for this dish as of 2024. trans_fat_g is reported as 0.1 g/100 g consistent with residual trans fats from commercial frying oils; this value is retained from the input record and is consistent with USDA data for commercially fried chicken products. Halal status is marked false as this cannot be confirmed without restaurant-level certification; consumers requiring halal-certified food should verify with the specific outlet. The input source URL (https://foodo.sg/) is a menu-listing platform and is not an approved nutrient source under Project Veritas v2; it has been replaced with USDA FoodData Central as the primary nutrient reference. GI of 65 is an analogous estimate; a direct GI measurement for Korean boneless pa dak has not been published in the University of Sydney GI Database or peer-reviewed literature as of 2024. Malay, Tamil, and Hindi local names and aliases have been added in iteration 3 to satisfy G6 multilingual naming compliance for this dish served in Singapore context.
